questions.text: What is the primary weakness of simple token-weighted voting?

options.text: It can lead to plutocracy, where wealthy token holders dominate decisions.
It requires all votes to be delegated.
It is too complex to implement.
It always requires a timelock.

questions.explanation: Simple token voting gives power proportional to wealth, which can lead to plutocracy - rule by the rich.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Simple token voting gives power proportional to wealth, which can lead to plutocracy - rule by the rich.

questions.text: In quadratic voting, if Alice holds 400 tokens, what is her voting power?
questions.explanation: Quadratic voting power = √(tokens held). √400 = 20. This compresses the power of large holders.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Quadratic voting power = √(tokens held). √400 = 20. This compresses the power of large holders.

questions.text: What is the primary benefit of conviction voting?

options.text: It prevents flash attacks by requiring tokens to be staked over time, making manipulation slow and expensive.
It allows proposals to pass instantly.
It eliminates the need for a treasury.
It makes all votes equal regardless of tokens.

questions.explanation: Conviction voting requires long-term staking, making it impossible for an attacker to quickly pass a malicious proposal.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Conviction voting requires long-term staking, making it impossible for an attacker to quickly pass a malicious proposal.

questions.text: In liquid democracy, what happens when Alice delegates her voting power to Bob?

options.text: Bob can vote on Alice's behalf, and Alice can revoke the delegation at any time.
Alice permanently loses her tokens.
Bob becomes the owner of Alice's tokens.
Alice can no longer vote directly on any proposal.

questions.explanation: Delegation is revocable and non-custodial. Alice retains ownership of her tokens and can vote directly or change her delegate at any time.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Delegation is revocable and non-custodial. Alice retains ownership of her tokens and can vote directly or change her delegate at any time.

questions.text: What is the main risk of reputation-based voting?

options.text: Early contributors may become entrenched, making it hard for newcomers to gain influence.
It is too fast for important decisions.
It requires purchasing tokens on the open market.
It makes the DAO fully centralized.

questions.explanation: Reputation systems can create an entrenched elite if early contributors accumulate irreversible reputation advantages.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Reputation systems can create an entrenched elite if early contributors accumulate irreversible reputation advantages.

questions.text: What problem does holographic consensus solve?

options.text: Proposal overload - too many proposals for the full DAO to evaluate efficiently.
The need for a treasury.
The inability to delegate votes.
The lack of a governance token.

questions.explanation: Holographic consensus uses prediction markets to filter proposals, so the full DAO only votes on proposals that have been "boosted" by stakers.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Holographic consensus uses prediction markets to filter proposals, so the full DAO only votes on proposals that have been "boosted" by stakers.

questions.text: Why are MST's low fees particularly beneficial for DAO governance?

options.text: They make complex voting mechanisms (quadratic, conviction) and frequent participation economically viable for all token holders.
They eliminate the need for a governance token.
They make DAOs legally compliant.
They prevent whale accumulation.

questions.explanation: On expensive chains, only whales can afford to vote regularly. MST's sub-paisa fees democratize participation.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

On expensive chains, only whales can afford to vote regularly. MST's sub-paisa fees democratize participation.

questions.text: Which governance model is best suited for a grant-giving DAO that distributes funds to community projects?

options.text: Conviction voting - it allows continuous proposal evaluation and prevents flash attacks on the treasury.
Simple token voting - it's the fastest.
Holographic consensus - it's the most complex.
Reputation-based - it requires no tokens.

questions.explanation: Conviction voting is ideal for treasury allocation because it requires sustained community support, preventing rushed or manipulated spending.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Conviction voting is ideal for treasury allocation because it requires sustained community support, preventing rushed or manipulated spending.

options.text: The minimum number of votes (or percentage of total token supply) that must participate for a proposal to be valid.
The maximum number of proposals per day.
The number of tokens required to delegate.
The time delay before execution.

questions.explanation: Quorum ensures that a minimum level of participation is reached, preventing a tiny active minority from making decisions for the entire DAO.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Quorum ensures that a minimum level of participation is reached, preventing a tiny active minority from making decisions for the entire DAO.

questions.text: Which of the following is an effective way to reduce the plutocracy problem in a DAO?

options.text: Use quadratic voting or delegation to amplify the influence of smaller token holders.
Remove the governance token entirely.
Give all power to a single admin.
Eliminate voting entirely.

questions.explanation: Quadratic voting and delegation are two proven mechanisms to reduce whale dominance without eliminating the capital alignment of token voting.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A

Quadratic voting and delegation are two proven mechanisms to reduce whale dominance without eliminating the capital alignment of token voting.

questions.text: Which of the following are weaknesses of simple token-weighted voting? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Plutocracy - large holders can dominate decisions.
Voter apathy - most token holders never vote.
Governance attacks via flash loans.
It is impossible to implement on a blockchain.

questions.explanation: All three are well-documented weaknesses of simple token voting. D is false - simple token voting is the easiest model to implement on-chain.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A, B, C

All three are well-documented weaknesses of simple token voting. D is false - simple token voting is the easiest model to implement on-chain.

questions.text: Which governance models help reduce whale dominance? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Quadratic voting.
Reputation-based voting.
Conviction voting (prevents flash attacks).
Simple token voting without any modifications.

questions.explanation: Quadratic voting compresses whale power; reputation-based voting disconnects power from capital; conviction voting prevents flash attacks. Simple token voting actually amplifies whale power.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A, B, C

Quadratic voting compresses whale power; reputation-based voting disconnects power from capital; conviction voting prevents flash attacks. Simple token voting actually amplifies whale power.

questions.text: Which of the following are components of liquid democracy? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Direct voting on proposals by token holders.
Delegation of voting power to trusted representatives.
Revocable delegation at any time.
Permanent transfer of tokens to the delegate.

questions.explanation: Liquid democracy allows direct voting, delegation, and revocation. It does NOT transfer token ownership - only voting power.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A, B, C

Liquid democracy allows direct voting, delegation, and revocation. It does NOT transfer token ownership - only voting power.

questions.text: What are the advantages of MST for implementing sophisticated DAO governance models? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Sub-paisa gas fees make frequent voting and complex on-chain calculations affordable.
Full EVM compatibility means OpenZeppelin Governor and other tools work out of the box.
High throughput ensures votes are processed quickly even during peak activity.
MST provides a built-in quadratic voting smart contract.

questions.explanation: MST offers low fees, EVM compatibility, and high throughput. However, quadratic voting contracts must be custom-built - they're not built into the chain.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A, B, C

MST offers low fees, EVM compatibility, and high throughput. However, quadratic voting contracts must be custom-built - they're not built into the chain.

questions.text: Which statements about conviction voting are TRUE? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Voting power grows over time as tokens remain staked on a proposal.
It prevents flash loan governance attacks.
C Users can withdraw their tokens from a proposal at any time, losing accumulated conviction.
Conviction can exceed the number of tokens staked.

questions.explanation: Conviction grows over time, prevents flash attacks, and is withdrawable. However, conviction is asymptotically bounded by the staked amount - it can never exceed it.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct: A, B, C

Conviction grows over time, prevents flash attacks, and is withdrawable. However, conviction is asymptotically bounded by the staked amount - it can never exceed it.
